trajectory_summary: This is a short, cooperative close-out call on a pre-warmed lead. Michael had already submitted his bills; Tyke's job was to convert that goodwill into a locked appointment, and he did. The call is flat and cooperative throughout — there is no arc of energy built or leaked, just a smooth procedural execution with a clean booking outcome. The primary coaching value is not in what went wrong but in the protocol gaps that create no-show risk on an otherwise solid sit.
outcome_dispute: v1 outcome tag is 'unknown' — this call should be tagged 'appointment_booked.' Michael confirmed 3:00 PM tomorrow, Tyke confirmed it back, and a reminder text was promised. The booking is clean.

primary_coaching_focus.quote: we will shoot your reminder text just a little bit beforehand, double check that time still works, so don't stress too much about this.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Tyke had a clean booking in hand — Michael double-confirmed the time — and then re-opened the commitment with 'double check that time still works.' Combined with the missing 'Is that fair?' handshake and the one-hour block omission, the button-up sequence has three gaps that compound. On a pre-warmed lead this may not cost the sit; on a colder lead, each gap is a no-show vector. The manager should use this call to drill the full button_up checklist as a non-negotiable close sequence, not a freestyle wind-down.

key_moments.quote: we ask you not be driving. Pretty self explanatory. We wanna make sure we're not distracting you while we're on the phone, as well as have access to your email since that's how we'll send you everything while we're on the phone with you so you can take a look at it yourself.
key_moments.stewart_read: Tyke hits the no-driving anchor correctly but substitutes 'email' for 'computer.' The schema requires the rep to specify that the customer should be at their computer to review the design. Email access on a phone is not the same as sitting at a computer — a customer who reads this as 'have your phone handy' may join the closer's call on mobile and be unable to view the design document, increasing no-show-equivalent risk (present but unqualified).

Michael's double confirmation is a strong behavioral signal of buy-in, but it is not a substitute for the verbal handshake. The schema is explicit: 'Is that fair?' is a required beat, predictive of show rate, and should not be skipped. Tyke never asked it. The double confirmation reflects Michael's existing goodwill — it does not replace the rep's job of eliciting a deliberate verbal commitment.

key_moments.quote: we will shoot your reminder text just a little bit beforehand, double check that time still works, so don't stress too much about this.
key_moments.stewart_read: The reminder text promise is a required button-up element and Tyke delivers it — but the framing 're-opens' the appointment. 'Double check that time still works' signals to Michael that the time is not yet locked, and 'don't stress too much about this' softens the commitment further. The schema's text follow-up is meant to confirm and anchor, not to offer a second opt-out window. This is a soft-optionality leak on an otherwise clean booking.

key_moments.quote: we are good to go in getting this, design built out for you. The last thing I needed to know was a good time for my specialist just to give you a call to go over that design as well as answer any questions or concerns you may have.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the strongest line in the call. Tyke converts the bill submission into forward momentum ('we are good to go'), personalizes the outcome ('design built out for you'), and frames the appointment as the natural next step rather than a new ask. The 'answer any questions or concerns' addition is a low-key curiosity hook — it signals value without over-explaining solar. Scope is held cleanly.
